data.title: Nuvalent deal delivers GSK’s first-ever lung cancer approval
data.description:
In a landmark moment for its oncology ambitions, GSK has secured FDA approval for Jideytro (zidesamtinib) as the first commercial lung cancer drug in the company’s history.

The milestone was made possible by GSK’s recent acquisition of Nuvalent for $10.6 billion. The approval for Jideytro comes just a week after the British pharma closed the deal and about two months ahead of the FDA’s target decision date of Sept. 18. 

The green light allows Jideytro to treat adult patients with advanced ROS1-positive non-small cell lung cancer who received a prior ROS1 inhibitor.

“The rapid progression of Jideytro from development to approval reflects both the strength of the underlying science and the urgent need for additional effective and tolerable treatments for ROS1-positive lung cancer,” GSK’s chief scientific officer, Tony Wood, said in a July 22 statement.

By GSK’s estimate, about 50,000 new cases of ROS1-positive NSCLC are diagnosed each year worldwide, mostly among non-smokers in their 40s and 50s who could stay on treatment for several years.

GSK has suggested that Jideytro holds “multi-blockbuster potential,” an ambitious projection considering none of the existing ROS1 NSCLC drugs—by Pfizer, Roche, Bristol Myers Squibb and most recently, Nuvation Bio—have come close to that target. 

Related

GSK to seek FDA approval for Jemperli in small but high-profile cancer use after phase 2 win

Jideytro differentiates itself from earlier therapies such as Pfizer’s Xalkori and BMS’ Augtyro through its unique molecular design. Nuvalent designed Jideytro to overcome treatment-emergent ROS1 mutations while sparing the structurally related TRK family, thereby preventing off-target toxicities. It features robust central nervous system penetration to tackle brain metastases, a common complication in ROS1-positive NSCLC. 

In the single-arm Arros-1 phase 1/2 clinical trial, Jideytro booked an objective response rate of 44% in 117 patients with ROS1 inhibitor-pretreated NSCLC. In terms of duration of response, 82% and 69% of responders remained in remission at six months and 12 months, respectively. 

Among 50 patients who had measurable brain metastases, responses were observed in 48% of patients, including 22% with a complete response, according to the drug’s label.

A 50% ORR rate was also recorded in the 42 patients who had ROS1 resistance mutations.

Despite the strong data, GSK has a long ways to go before Jideytro could reach blockbuster status. One of its primary competitors is Ibtrozi (taletrectinib) by David Hung’s Nuvation Bio. Following a line-agnostic FDA nod for ROS1-positive NSCLC in June 2025, Ibtrozi—which also features intracranial activity and TRK sparing—generated $18.5 million during the first quarter of 2026.

The current Jideytro label only allows the GSK drug to be used in patients who received a prior ROS1 inhibitor. Responding to a Fierce inquiry, a GSK spokesperson pointed to Nuvalent’s previous comment that it would seek a first-line label expansion this year. 

At the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meeting, Nuvalent also reported promising preliminary data for zidesamtinib in non-NSCLC solid tumors, indicating the drug may hold pan-tumor potential with a 40% ORR across 10 tumor types. 

Jideytro is the first product emerging from GSK’s Nuvalent acquisition. Neladalkib, a fourth-generation ALK inhibitor, could be next with an FDA target action date of Nov. 27, 2026, for advanced ALK-positive NSCLC. GSK has floated multi-blockbuster hopes for that drug, too.

Beyond the Nuvalent assets, GSK is advancing risvutatug rezetecan (ris-rez), a B7-H3-targeted antibody-drug conjugate, in a phase 3 study in extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer. The drug’s original developer, China’s Hansoh Pharma, just reported a phase 3 win in SCLC in China.

data.title: Sanofi gets FDA untitled letter over Beyfortus promotional emails
data.description:
The FDA has called out Sanofi’s promotion of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) antibody Beyfortus in a new untitled letter, taking issue with the company’s characterization of the med as a general RSV preventative. 

As the FDA explains in the letter (PDF), Beyfortus’ 2023 U.S. approval specifically clears the drug as a defense against RSV lower respiratory tract disease. Sanofi’s promotional emails (PDF) regarding Beyfortus, on the other hand, urge healthcare professionals to “help prevent RSV disease in infants,” creating what the FDA’s Office of Prescription Drug Promotion (OPDP) deems a “misleading impression” about the drug’s indication.

Specifically, the Sanofi emails seem to point to Beyfortus' use as a general RSV preventative, “including both upper and lower respiratory tract disease, when this is not the case,” the FDA explained.  

In its promotional emails (PDF) flagged by the FDA, the company refers to Beyfortus as a monoclonal antibody to help prevent “RSV disease” numerous times. Although Sanofi does mention Beyfortus’ official indication to prevent RSV lower respiratory tract infection further down in the emails—and also notes the indication in full in its ‘indication’ section—the agency maintains that the “presentation of this information is not sufficient to correct the overall misleading impression created by these claims.” 

The letter asks that Sanofi take “immediate action” to address the concerns and submit a written response within 15 working days from date of receipt. 

A Sanofi spokesperson acknowledged the untitled letter in an emailed statement to Fierce Pharma Marketing, noting that the company takes “this matter seriously" and is "reviewing the letter carefully.”

“Sanofi maintains strict regulatory compliance policies for its promotional communications. We are committed to ensuring that all promotional materials for Beyfortus are accurate, balanced, and fully compliant with FDA regulations. We will respond to the agency within the required timeframe,” the spokesperson added. 

Related

FDA issues untitled letter after finding ‘eye-catching graphics’ in promotional emails

The new OPDP letter, released to the public on July 22 and dated July 15, comes as the company faces separate marketing backlash in the U.K. from the country's drug marketing watchdog, the PMCPA. 

On July 21, the PMCPA hit Sanofi with a public reprimand for “very serious breaches” of the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry (ABPI) code based on its Beyfortus claims across the pond. The case goes back to a 2024 article in which Sanofi’s former CEO Paul Hudson was quoted saying that the U.K.'s national RSV vaccine program should consider Beyfortus instead of Pfizer’s Abrysvo, which led Pfizer to contact the PMCPA over the article and two others, plus a press release. 

Sanofi could still face further sanctions over the matter in the U.K. and is to provide the PMCPA with a copy of a third-party audit report it has arranged for. The high level of regulatory scurrility over the case stems from the PMCPA’s conclusion that the company’s claims could “undermine the public’s trust in vaccination campaigns” and spark decreasing vaccine rates and public health concerns. 

Last year, Sanofi’s global Beyfortus sales totaled 1.78 billion euros ($2.03 billion), representing 5.6% growth from 2024. The company co-developed the med with AstraZeneca and brought it to the U.S. market in July 2023. The duo shares the RSV-LRTI prevention market with Merck and its Enflonsia, while Pfizer’s Abrysvo can be used as a maternal vaccine in pregnant women to prevent RSV in children from birth.

data.title: Commercial Evidence Is the Next Pharma Intelligence Breakthrough
data.description:
Clinical teams have access to a wealth of data, most of it structured and peer-reviewed, from which they're able to mine insights about how a given medicine performs in a defined patient population under controlled conditions. Real-world evidence (RWE), accumulated via electronic health records, patient registries and insurance claims, extends the clinical side’s data advantage even further.

The question RWE helps answer is how a drug works in the real world. Until recently, commercial teams did not have a similar way of determining whether the market understood the value of their therapies, and if not, where they were falling short.

Now they do. The next era of life sciences commercialization will be defined by Commercial Evidence, which compiles not what brands are broadcasting but what markets are actually saying. It consists of the precise language patients or HCPs use to ask questions, frees commercial groups to operate like clinical teams, making decisions on the basis of what the evidence shows.

With Commercial Evidence, teams are tapping into information that’s more consequential: A compliant, usable record of what HCPs, patients, caregivers and field teams are actually saying at all stages of the process.

Why the Old Commercial Playbook Falls Short

The traditional brand playbook is currently inadequate. The infrastructure underlying many commercial programs was created when the overarching goal was to reach as many physicians as possible with a consistent message, measuring success by the reach and frequency. Even today, many commercial teams are still counting clicks and impressions, then inputting the data into dashboards that offer tidy, snapshot views.

This system fails today’s teams because website clicks and a few rigid drop-down menus cannot capture the nuance of real conversations. 

"When you are driving a paradigm shift in a therapeutic category, legacy data is a rearview mirror," says Graham Goodrich, Chief Commercial Officer at Apnimed, Inc. "Success relies on identifying precisely where clinical inertia and patient friction live in real time. Moving from traditional metrics to Commercial Evidence gives us the insights needed to remove barriers to treatment."

Powered by voice agents that convert spoken notes into text and agents that can analyze free-text fields, Commercial Evidence changes this.

For years, some of the richest signals in healthcare went uncaptured. When patients interact with a care navigator or physicians pose questions, these natural, unscripted exchanges generate the language that structured systems were never designed to record: An untrackable flow of dialogue revealing what people actually think, ask, and worry about.

How Conversational AI Unlocks the Signal

By interpreting conversations at scale on brand websites and email, Conversational AI transforms that lost dialogue into a compliant, analyzable record – and, in doing so, provides actionable Commercial Evidence. What was once dismissed as too subjective, messy or risky to inform crucial decisions is now a usable asset, reflecting a broader shift in how the industry regards open text.

Now commercial teams can see what physicians and patients are asking. They can base their decisions on what HCPs, patients, caregivers, field teams and other stakeholders across the medical marketing spectrum are actually saying and doing.

No longer do they have to make educated guesses about why a message failed to land, or why a physician may be reluctant to prescribe a product with credible data. They can determine where in the patient journey confusion or frustration leads to them leaving a site without an answer, as well as where HCP objections don’t find their way into a rep’s dashboard.

Commercialization is a chain of conversations: A physician objection in the field, reimbursement confusion in the support channel, patient questions in service interactions. Once open text is classified, summarized and compared at scale, those fragments move from anecdotes to evidence.

"For too long, commercial teams have operated under a top-down, broadcasting mindset," says BJ Jones, Chief Commercial Officer at NewAmsterdam Pharma. "Commercial Evidence flips that dynamic, forcing an organization to shift toward a true listening culture. When you align your strategy around the actual language patients and physicians use when they are stuck, you build a patient-centric commercial model."

Putting Commercial Evidence to Work

Commercial Evidence should be applied across multiple places in the commercialization journey. Prior to launch, it can show whether the market actually understands your story in the language the market uses. During launch, it can surface recurring issues by specialty, region or channel before they progress to underperformance. After launch, it can identify where education is failing, where onboarding breaks or where access barriers persist.

For brand teams, that exposes critical content gaps, helping them understand exactly what patients and HCPs are searching for but cannot find. Reps can arm themselves with direct evidence of what a specific HCP was researching prior to a visit. Patient support teams can fine-tune their messaging to address the exact verbiage used by patients to show where they don’t have complete information.

Commercial Evidence means more data, yes, but it also means more actionable knowledge and visibility, which ultimately gives commercial teams the ability to consider evidence of misunderstanding, hesitation or friction. It’s pushing commercial teams closer to the standard the clinical teams have experienced for years.

Written by Chase Feiger, MD

data.title: Sanofi reprimanded for disparaging Pfizer’s RSV vaccine
data.description:
A U.K. marketing watchdog has reprimanded Sanofi for using “a highly misleading interpretation of data” to make disparaging claims about Pfizer’s respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccine.

The PMCPA, the self-regulatory body that oversees drug marketing in the U.K., took Sanofi to task over three cases brought by Pfizer. One case, details of which were published in February, related to quotes attributed to Paul Hudson, then Sanofi’s CEO, in a U.K. newspaper. After complaining about the article to the PMCPA, Pfizer filed subsequent complaints about two other news stories and a Sanofi press release.

In all three cases, the PMCPA found Sanofi had brought discredit upon, and reduced confidence in, the pharmaceutical industry. The censure, the harshest in the PMCPA’s arsenal, led the watchdog to publicly reprimand Sanofi and run adverts publicizing the company’s wrongdoing.

Sanofi could face further sanctions. The PMCPA discussed whether to audit Sanofi but opted to defer its decision on potential further sanctions until after its appeal board meets in October. The watchdog has told Sanofi to send a senior representative to the meeting. 

The company escaped the immediate imposition of a PMCPA audit after telling the watchdog that it had arranged a third-party review of its operations for June and July. The PMCPA expects Sanofi to provide a copy of the third-party audit report and share details of actions it has taken, and is taking, in response to the findings. 

At a minimum, the PMCPA expects the third-party report to cover Sanofi’s culture, compliance program, adherence to the U.K. marketing code and implementation of standard operating practices. Sanofi told the PMCPA that the issues exposed by its comments in the Sunday Express, Health Service Journal and two social media posts were limited to one business unit and a short period of time. 

There has been a significant change in leadership personnel since Pfizer complained about the materials in August, and governance and process improvements have been implemented to avoid similar breaches occurring in future, Sanofi said. The third-party report could give the PMCPA a window into whether Sanofi’s operations support its claims. 

The level of scrutiny reflects the PMCPA’s conclusion that Sanofi made misleading claims, across different media outlets, that could undermine public trust in vaccination campaigns. After the Observer quoted Hudson as saying that the U.K. selected Pfizer’s Abrysvo over Sanofi’s Beyfortus on financial grounds, the PMCPA accused the French drugmaker of again undermining the vaccine program in two other articles. 

The PMCPA published its report on the two articles alongside the outcome of Pfizer’s complaint about a global Sanofi press release. Sanofi’s press release compared real-world RSV hospitalization rates in Spain and the U.K. to make the case that Beyfortus has a “public health advantage” over Abrysvo. The PMCPA deemed the comparison misleading, driving it to rule that Sanofi brought discredit on the industry.

data.title: Novartis CEO shrugs off oral PCSK9 threat amid Q2 growth
data.description:
Novartis' CEO remains unfazed by the threat of a newly approved oral PCSK9 inhibitor in Merck & Co.’s Lipfendra, doubling down on the long-term potential of his company's injectable cholesterol drug, Leqvio.

Speaking on the company’s Q2 earnings call, Novartis CEO Vas Narasimhan brushed off fears that daily pills could stall Leqvio. As Novartis moves past key patent expirations to deliver a return to top-line growth—partly thanks to strong performance from Leqvio—Narasimhan argued that Leqvio’s twice yearly dosing offers a unique opportunity.

“Even with orals launching, our opportunity remains for driving strong growth in the segment that wants infrequently administered, physician-administered medicines for lipid lowering in the United States, and we see this as an attractive and growing segment that supports our peak sales potential in the U.S. and beyond,” Narasimhan said.

Novartis has pegged Leqvio peak sales at above $4 billion, based on the fact that many of the more than 70 million U.S. patients with elevated LDL cholesterol were not able to reach their lipid-lowering targets with older treatments.

Initially struggling with a slow launch as doctors got accustomed to the “buy-and-bill” reimbursement model, Leqvio reached blockbuster status in 2025 with $1.2 billion sales. In this year's second quarter, the small interfering RNA therapy grew sales by 59% at constant currencies, reaching $480 million, landing 5% above analysts’ expectations.

In the U.S., Narasimhan highlighted monthly total prescription growth of 49%, as Leqvio “outpaced the advanced lipid-lowering market.” With Medicare Part B uses, which the CEO said represent Leqvio’s most important segment, Novartis currently keeps a 23.3% share, up 3.6% year to date.  

With Lipfendra’s launch, the buy-and-bill pathway for physician-administered drugs now works in Leqvio’s favor, insulating the Novartis med from the potentially brutal price battle between orals and self-injected antibody therapies, Narasimhan noted.

Related

Merck scores at FDA as Lipfendra becomes world's first oral PCSK9 treatment

Outside the U.S., Narasimhan predicts Leqvio could become Novartis’ largest medicine ever in China, even ahead of the company’s megablockbuster heart failure treatment Entresto.

Following its recent inclusion in China’s National Reimbursement Drug List, Leqvio has now doubled its market share, with strong performance in both the hospital setting and other market segments, according to Narasimhan. 

“We think there’s an opportunity in cardiovascular, hypertension and [cardiovascular] risk reduction for our follow-on siRNAs in China, where there seems to be a high demand for infrequently administered therapies with very clean safety profiles,” Narasimhan said. 

To potentially further bolster Leqvio’s clinical profile, Novartis is on track to report data from two cardiovascular outcomes study in 2027.

Leqvio is among several new medicines that drove Novartis’ Q2 sales to 1% growth at constant exchange rates, a surprisingly strong showing coming off of a 5% decline in Q1. 

Entresto, which was once Novartis’ top-selling drug, continued to see significant sales erosions thanks to its U.S. loss of exclusivity. Sales from the drug plummeted 51% in Q2, tumbling below $1.2 billion and missing Wall Street consensus estimates by 9%.

Related

Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Novartis reconnects to its past as it moves forward

In contrast, immunotherapy asset Cosentyx chalked up a 10% increase at an unchanged exchange rate, reaching $1.8 billion. Breast cancer drug Kisqali brought in $1.7 billion sales in Q2, representing 43% year-over-year growth, and Kesimpta’s $1.4 billion sales marked a 32% jump. 

Despite a 5% overall groupwide topline beat versus consensus, Novartis maintained its full-year sales guidance. As CFO Mukul Mehta noted on Tuesday’s call, the company’s Q2 revenue benefited 1 percentage point from some “one-time phasing items, which will reverse in the second half.”

data.title: Novo accuses Lilly of running ‘false and misleading’ GLP-1 ads
data.description:
Novo Nordisk has accused rival Eli Lilly of running ad campaigns containing “deliberately false” messages that mislead the public about the comparative efficacy of GLP-1 medicines. 

In a lawsuit filed Tuesday in New Jersey, the Danish drugmaker said the “false and misleading advertising is causing irreparable harm to Novo Nordisk and to the patients Lilly deceives.” Novo wants the court to order Lilly to stop making the contested claims and to “conduct a corrective advertising campaign” to tell consumers that earlier ads “conveyed a deceptive comparison” of the companies’ medicines. 

The case centers on the doses of Novo drugs that Lilly studied in clinical trials and used as comparators in its ads. Novo cited a Lilly TV ad to support its argument. A voiceover and on-screen text stated that, on average, people on Lilly’s Zepbound lost 50 pounds, compared to 33 pounds on Novo’s Wegovy.

The Wegovy data cover patients who received a 2.4-mg dose. Novo won FDA approval for a 7.2-mg dose, which helped patients lose 47 pounds, in March. Lilly’s ad features text at the bottom of the screen stating that the 7.2-mg dose wasn’t tested in the study comparing Zepbound to Wegovy but has since been approved. The ad’s disclaimer failed to appease Novo. 

“That footnote is ambiguous, confusing, virtually illegible and wholly inadequate,” Novo said. “Among other flaws, it does nothing to communicate that the new, higher dose of Wegovy is substantially more effective than the doses included in the study, and therefore the ad’s message claiming dramatic superiority of Zepbound is not supportable.”

A Lilly spokesperson defended the company's ads, telling Fierce Pharma via email that the data come from a gold-standard study that is "the only head-to-head, randomized clinical trial directly comparing tirzepatide and semaglutide in weight management." Lilly hit back at Novo.

"Rather than compete on the merits of its products, Novo is asking a court to stop Lilly from communicating the results of that trial," the spokesperson said. "We stand firmly behind our advertising. It is truthful, it is transparent and it is grounded in the most direct scientific evidence available—exactly what patients deserve. We will continue to focus on the science and defend against this lawsuit vigorously.”

Novo also attacked Lilly’s comparisons of Mounjaro and Ozempic, the brand names for the companies’ GLP-1 medicines in diabetes. The lawsuit notes a national TV ad that presented data from a study that compared Mounjaro 15 mg to Ozempic 1 mg. Like in obesity, Novo won approval for a higher dose of Ozempic—2-mg—after the study. Lilly noted the availability of the 2-mg dose in a footnote to its ad. 

The lawsuit seeks to turn the “scale and reach” of Lilly’s campaign against the U.S. company. Since April 27, Lilly’s revised Zepbound TV ad has received more than 700 million impressions, Novo said. The breadth of the campaign means the messages reach a large audience, and the competitive harm to Novo “is amplified with each day Lilly’s false and misleading advertising continues to run,” the lawsuit claimed. 

Communicating the messages across major broadcast and cable networks and through sponsored social media advertising on TikTok and Facebook is particularly harmful because consumers rely on ads to form their understanding of medicines, Novo said. The drugmaker claimed that: “Lilly’s strategy is to deprive consumers of their right to accurate, up-to-date and complete information.”

The lawsuit starts a new, more combative phase of Novo and Lilly’s battle for the GLP-1 market. While Novo had a first-mover advantage, Lilly is now the market leader. Mounjaro sales hit (PDF) $8.7 billion in the first quarter, compared to (PDF) 27.8 billion Danish krone ($4.2 billion) for Ozempic. Zepbound outsold Wegovy over the same period.  

Editor's note: This story has been updated with a statement from Eli Lilly.

data.title: J&J’s Tremfya remains on top in Q2 pharma ad spending league
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson looks set to continue battling AbbVie over ad spend in the second half of 2026 as the U.S. Big Pharma’s immunology drug takes the top spot for a second quarter in a row, according to data from iSpot.tv.

J&J’s immunology med Tremfya saw spend for Q2 hit $115.3 million, beating out AbbVie’s rival Skyrizi at $108.7 million. 

This kept Tremfya in its number one spot for the second quarter in a row. Skyrizi, however, moved up one spot quarter over quarter, displacing AbbVie’s sibling immunology therapy Rinvoq, which dropped down to third position.

The amount spent on Tremfya was nevertheless massively down from the $169.1 million J&J spent in Q1, while AbbVie coughed up $16.2 million less on Skyrizi ads over the same period. 

That was something of a trend: Nine of the top 10 saw their spending on ads drop from the first to the second quarter.

The only pharma that charted a spending increase for the period was Teva with its Huntington’s disease chorea or tardive dyskinesia drug Austedo, though the boost was a scant $1 million quarter over quarter. This saw the medication reach the top 10 rankings, an achievement Teva could not claim back in Q1. 

Check out the top 10 biggest pharma ad spenders for the second quarter below. 

 

1. Tremfya 

Movement: No movement vs. Q1 2026

What is it? Johnson & Johnson’s immunology drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $115.3 million (down from $169.1 million in Q1 2026)

  • UC/Crohn’s disease: $64.1 million
  • Psoriasis: $4.7 million
  • Psoriatic Arthritis: $46.6 million

Top TV network by spend share of voice (SOV): ABC (20.93%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: Good Morning America (5.35%)

Number of spots: Nine (two psoriasis, four UC/Crohn’s disease, three psoriatic arthritis)

Biggest-ticket ad: “Relentless Weed” (est. $51 million)

 

2. Skyrizi  

Movement: Up one spot

What is it? AbbVie’s immunology drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $108.7 million (down from $124.9 million in Q1 2026)

  • UC/Crohn’s disease: $56 million
  • Psoriasis: $46.1 million
  • Psoriatic arthritis: $6.6 million

Top TV network by spend SOV: ABC (19.90%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: NBA (4.45%)

Number of spots: Six (two UC/Crohn’s disease, three psoriasis, one psoriatic arthritis) 

Biggest-ticket ad: “I'm All In: Swimming Pool” (est. $36.4 million)

 

3. Rinvoq 

Movement: Down one spot

What is it? AbbVie’s JAK immunology drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $91.2 million (down from $129.3 million in Q1 2026)

  • UC/Crohn’s disease: $36.8 million
  • Eczema: $26.2 million
  • Arthritis: $28.2 million

Top TV network by spend SOV: ABC (23.17%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: Today (2.89%) 

Number of spots:  Six (two UC/Crohn’s disease, two arthritis, two eczema) 

Biggest-ticket ad: “Classic Car, Tennis and Fire Station” (est. $36.7 million)

 

4. Zepbound 

Movement: No change

What is it?  Eli Lilly’s obesity drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $67.8 million (down from $74.9 million in Q1 2026)

Top TV network by spend SOV: CBS (21.36%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: NBA (14.07%)

Number of spots: 11 

Biggest-ticket ad: “Watch This: Data” (est. $16.7 million)

 

5. Caplyta

Movement: No change

What is it? Johnson & Johnson’s atypical antipsychotic drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $55.6 million (down from $67.8 in Q1 2026)

  • MDD: $53.1 million
  • Bipolar: $2.5 million

Top TV network by spend SOV: ABC (14.93%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: Good Morning America (4.57%) 

Number of spots: Four (one bipolar, three MDD)

Biggest-ticket ad: “Let in the Lyte: More of the Lyte” (est. $19.3 million)

 

6. Rexulti (depression)

Movement: Up two spots

What is it? Lundbeck and Otsuka’s neurology drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $47.3 million (down from $52.3 million in Q1 2026)

Top TV network by spend SOV: CBS (14.49%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: NCIS (4.20%)

Number of spots: Two 

Biggest-ticket ad: “Progress” (est. $47.3 million)

 

7. Dupixent

Movement: No change

What is it? Sanofi and Regeneron’s immunology drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $43.8 million (down from $52.6 million in Q1 2026)

  • Eczema: $26.3 million
  • Asthma: $17.4 million
  • Gastrointestinal: $97K

Top TV network by spend SOV: ABC (30.37%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: NBA (9.45%) 

Number of spots: Eight (three asthma, four eczema, one gastrointestinal) 

Biggest-ticket ad: “Feel the Heal” (est. $15.5 million)

 

8. Jardiance

Movement: Up one spot

What is it? Eli Lilly and Boehringer Ingelheim’s diabetes and kidney disease drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $36.7 million (down from $51.9 million in Q1 2026)

  • CKD: $9.5 million
  • Diabetes: $27.2 million

Top TV network by spend SOV: ABC (18.16%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: NBA (5.96%)

Number of spots: Three (two CKD, one diabetes)

Biggest-ticket ad: “Behind the Scenes” (est. $27.2 million)

 

9. Vraylar 

Movement: Up one spot

What is it? AbbVie’s atypical antipsychotic

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $32.8 million (down from $40.8 million in Q1 2026)

  • Depression: $22.9 million
  • Bipolar: $9.9 million

Top TV network by spend SOV: CBS (11.93%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: ABC World News Tonight With David Muir (4.11%)

Number of spots: Three (one bipolar, two depression)

Biggest-ticket ad: “Jane’s Head” (est. $22.7 million)

 

10. Austedo

Movement: Not listed in Q1 2026

What is it? Teva’s Huntington’s disease chorea or tardive dyskinesia drug

Est. national linear TV ad spend: $27.7 million (up from $26.7 million in Q1 2026)

Top TV network by spend SOV: CBS (29.51%)

Top TV program by spend SOV: Today With Jenna & Sheinelle (7.12%)

Number of spots: Two

Biggest-ticket ad: “Derek” (est. $14.6 million)

data.title: Sanofi survey reveals diabetes misconceptions and screening gaps
data.description:
Widespread misconceptions about Type 1 diabetes could be contributing to a reluctance to get screened for the autoimmune disease, according to a survey Sanofi commissioned.

Working with the nonprofit Beyond Type 1, Sanofi tasked Ipsos with surveying U.S. adults who are at risk of developing Type 1 diabetes and caregivers for at-risk children. The collaborators augmented data from the 1,000-person survey with insights from interviews of 511 adults and caregivers in California, Florida, Georgia, Massachusetts, New York and Texas.

Almost two-thirds of respondents said Type 1 diabetes is primarily diagnosed in children, despite adults accounting for more than half of new cases. One-fifth of respondents said autoantibody screening isn’t necessary for adults. 

Limited awareness of adult-onset Type 1 diabetes is one of several barriers to screening observed in the survey. The survey found a disconnect between the proportion of people concerned about their or their child’s Type 1 diabetes risk—82%—and the number planning to get screened. When asked about the next six months, 46% of respondents said they either won’t get screened or are undecided whether to do so.

The survey found 85% of respondents felt more likely to screen once they learned that they were at a heightened risk of developing the disease. Respondents named a doctor’s recommendation and learning more about the screening process as the top two motivators for getting tested.  

Sanofi received an expanded FDA label for Type 1 diabetes treatment Tzield last month. The drug, which is used to delay disease progression, is part of a portfolio of Type 1 diabetes drugs that includes insulin products such as Toujeo. Sanofi reported (PDF) Tzield sales of 14 million euros ($16 million) in the first quarter.

The survey’s focus on screening continues Sanofi’s interest in the topic. Last year, the drugmaker signed up model Chrissy Teigen for a campaign to encourage people to screen early for Type 1 diabetes. Teigen’s 6-year-old son, Miles, was hospitalized with complications of the autoimmune disease.

data.title: J&J, Time to name Healthcare Champion of the Year
data.description:
After nearly a century of naming its Person of the Year each December, Time is adding another prestigious recognition with help from Johnson & Johnson.

J&J and Time are teaming up to honor a Healthcare Champion of the Year, to be chosen by J&J and a panel of industry experts and recognized at Time’s “A Year in TIME” celebration in New York City in December.  Nominations for the award opened July 16 and will close August 28. 

The branded award will go to someone who is “shaping the future of health,” J&J explained in a press release, spotlighting impact and highlighting “what becomes possible when healthcare professionals are supported and empowered to lead change where it matters most.”

More specifically, the judging panel is looking for someone who demonstrates excellence across three core factors: grit, innovation and care, J&J further laid out on its dedicated Healthcare Champion of the Year website. 

The nominees should have proven “leadership, resilience and determination” in chasing solutions to complex health challenges and be able to advance care through new, fresh approaches, system improvement and otherwise “accelerating progress” for patients or communities. In addition, the healthcare champion must hold compassion and a deep commitment to patients. 

“Healthcare professionals bring skill, compassion and resolve to some of the most important moments in people’s lives,” J&J’s EVP of global corporate affairs Vanessa Broadhurst elaborated in the release. “With the Healthcare Champion of the Year Award, we proudly recognize those whose compassion, innovation, and grit are transforming patient care today and building a healthier tomorrow.”

The nomination pool is open to healthcare professionals across roles, including physicians, nurses, surgeons and the like who are currently employed in the U.S. and actively involved in clinical care or care delivery. J&J employees are excluded from nominations, as are Time employees and their immediate family members. People can nominate themselves or someone else, with the individual's consent, and the application should focus on written responses that include examples of grit, innovation or care that have led to tangible results or outcomes. 

Related

Gilead's Yeztugo, Eisai's Leqembi Iqlik, Vertex's Journavx among 2025's best inventions: Time

In their applications, prospective nominees are asked to explain “why this work matters now.” As J&J noted in its release, healthcare workers are navigating some of the “most consequential moments in healthcare,” making strong leadership at strained health systems more important than ever.

Time’s historic Person of the Year award most often goes to political figures or other major stewards of culture, with the most recent healthcare-related winner emerging in 2014 with the “Ebola fighters” of the global epidemic that year. 

However, biopharma products regularly earn recognition on Time’s annual “Best Inventions” list. The 2025 honorees included Ars Pharma’s epinephrine nasal spray Neffy; Eisai and Biogen’s Iqlik autoinjector, which delivers subcutaneous maintenance doses of Leqembi; Gilead’s twice-yearly HIV PrEP med Yeztugo; and Vertex’s nonopioid pain drug Journavx.

J&J, meanwhile, looks to empower nurses and community health workers through its J&J CareCommunity social impact platform, which focuses on “closing the gap between communities and care.” Over the past three years, the company has reached 2.3 million healthcare workers through these efforts, which include collaborations with several advocacy and service groups such as the Global Surgery Foundation, Lions Club International Foundation and more.

data.title: Novartis reconnects to its past as it moves forward
data.description:
In the past six years, the pharmaceutical industry has sought to show itself to the world more than ever before. The COVID-19 pandemic brought these companies into the spotlight and, rather than shy away, many Big Pharmas decided to grab the mic and talk up their companies. 

Coupled with that was a growing trend in which companies reinvigorated their public-facing brands. This included multi-million-dollar rebranding efforts incorporating new logos, reimagined content, websites and refreshed corporate outlooks, with some even adopting new names. 

Novartis ranked among those making changes and, back in 2023, created a new color design for its corporate backgrounds. 

But the Swiss pharma stopped short of radical rebranding that swept through the likes of GSK, Sanofi, Johnson & Johnson and Pfizer, as the industry sought to jump on its reputation boost during the pandemic and be more like its tech industry cousin in talking itself up.

Now, to help celebrate its 30th anniversary, Novartis is tapping the colors, culture and successes of its past to help create a brand evolution that guides its future. 

Related

Novartis follows Pfizer, GSK, J&J and Sanofi as it launches a fresh corporate rebranding

Modern-day Novartis traces its roots back to 1996 when the company was created through the merger of Ciba-Geigy and Sandoz.

The company also boasts a deeper heritage stretching back 250 years to a chemical, dyes and materials company in Basel, Switzerland, where the pharma still calls home. That history in dyes has also helped shape today’s color palette. 

“I think it's less of a big bang than some of the other [pharma] companies that have been doing these like big unveilings of a new brand,” said Michelle Weese, Chief Corporate Affairs Officer, Novartis, in an interview with Fierce Pharma Marketing. 

“For us, it's really a visit to our history and our heritage. None of it is new. We’re just reconnecting, I think, in some ways, with what's always been at the heart of us.” 

As part of the branding, Novartis “revisited the color palette” of old, focused on tones of orange. “We saw that there was a lot of warmth and energy in that era, and so we just decided to highlight that to be able to bring forward more of the emotion that comes with what we do for a living,” Weese said.

And for Novartis, that is creating new medicines, extending lives and managing health. More and more, Big Pharma is looking to espouse this messaging: That its impact on human health and biology is its defining feature. 

Weese said Novartis has, as part of this process, “gone back through our archives”—something that has “given us the ability to just look back in history, pick moments that were really meaningful and allow us to celebrate them.”

She said the unique factor here is that Novartis “is really leaning into our brands, leaning into the accomplishments, and bridging that with the technology of the future to bring more hope to patients.

“What I would say is [Novartis’ refreshed branding] is different than the other brands because it has real stories behind it.”
